articles.title: The Crocodile Relative That Switched from Four Legs to Two as It Grew Up
articles.summary: Imagine a poodle-sized reptile that starts life crawling on all fours, then gradually shifts to walking on two legs as an adult. That's exactly what scientists discovered about Sonselasuchus cedrus, a newly described crocodile relative from the Late Triassic. By analyzing fossilized leg bones from a massive dig in Arizona's Petrified Forest, researchers found that its hind limbs grew much longer and more robust than its forelimbs as it aged. This "peculiar" creature had a toothless beak, hollow bones, and large eye sockets—features that look like those of ornithomimid dinosaurs, but evolved independently. The study, published in the Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology, reveals how some ancient animals changed their movement style as they matured.

articles.title: Venus Just Gave Up a Secret: A Lava Tube Bigger Than Any on Earth
articles.summary: By reanalyzing radar data from NASA's Magellan probe from the 1990s, scientists have discovered the first lava tube ever detected on Venus. The tube, located near the volcano Nyx Mons, is at least 375 meters deep and could be up to 1 kilometer wide—larger than any lava tube on Earth or Mars. A collapsed "skylight" about 150 meters deep revealed its presence. This finding, published in Nature Communications, raises new questions about how such a massive tube formed under Venus's Earth-like gravity. It also highlights the lasting value of the Magellan mission and will guide future probes like NASA's VERITAS and the European Space Agency's EnVision, both set to launch before 2031.

articles.title: 289-Million-Year-Old Mummy Reptile Reveals the Origins of How We Breathe
articles.summary: A remarkably preserved fossil of a small reptile called Captorhinus aguti, found in an Oklahoma cave, has revealed the earliest known example of rib-based breathing in land animals. The 289-million-year-old specimen still has three-dimensional skin, calcified cartilage, and even traces of ancient proteins—nearly 100 million years older than any previously found. Using neutron computed tomography, scientists reconstructed its breathing system: muscles between the ribs expanded the chest cavity to pull air into the lungs. This innovation, known as costal aspiration, was a crucial evolutionary step that allowed early amniotes (the group including reptiles, birds, and mammals) to become more active and dominate land ecosystems. The study was published in Nature.
